questions.prompt: When does Vercel typically win vs self-hosting?

choices.text: Most teams under 50 engineers — Vercel's tight Next.js integration (git deploys, branch previews, edge network, automatic image optimization, ISR caching, serverless Server Actions, edge middleware) saves dramatically more engineering time than its bill costs. Self-hosting requires reverse proxy setup, CDN, image optimization, monitoring, CI/CD, SSL — each a chunk of work. Total cost of ownership including engineering hours almost always favors Vercel until you hit specific triggers (compliance requirements, on-prem deployments, extreme cost optimization at very high traffic, custom infrastructure needs).

questions.explanation: Vercel wins on engineering simplicity for most teams. Self-host for specific needs.

questions.prompt: Started on Vercel; might need to migrate later. How to keep options open?

choices.text: Design for portability. Don't depend on Vercel-specific environment quirks. Use abstractions for image storage (e.g., S3-compatible interfaces), databases (Postgres anywhere), queues (BullMQ + Redis vs Vercel-only Inngest). Keep state in your own database, not Vercel KV unless you're committed. Pin Node version explicitly in package.json engines. If you stay on Vercel, no cost; if you migrate later, the work is a containerization + CDN + reverse proxy setup rather than a full rewrite. Open-source PaaS options like Coolify, Dokku, and Caprover bridge if you want PaaS DX on your own infrastructure.

questions.explanation: Use portable abstractions; pin Node; avoid Vercel-only storage. Migration becomes containerization, not rewrite.
